         left++;

      }

      max_len = max(max_len, right - left + 1);

   }

   return max_len;

}

Now, code in C++:

class Solution {
public:
    int maxConsecutiveAnswers(string answerKey, int k) {
        int maxT = 0, countF = 0, left = 0;
        for (int right = 0; right < answerKey.size(); ++right) {
            if (answerKey[right] == 'F') countF++;
            while (countF > k) {
                if (answerKey[left] == 'F') countF--;
                left++;
            }
            maxT = max(maxT, right - left + 1);
        }
        int maxF = 0, countT = 0, left2 = 0;
        for (int right = 0; right < answerKey.size(); ++right) {
            if (answerKey[right] == 'T') countT++;
            while (countT > k) {
                if (answerKey[left2] == 'T') countT--;
                left2++;
            }
            maxF = max(maxF, right - left2 + 1);
        }
        return max(maxT, maxF);
    }
};

Yes, that's the code. Now check for possible edge cases.

What if k=0? Well, the code works. For example, if k=0, then you can't flip anything. So the code finds the maximum consecutive same letters.

Another example: answerKey is "TTT", k=0. For 'T' case, countF is 0, so maxT is 3. For 'F' case, countT is 3. Since k=0, the while loop will trigger if countT>0. Another edge case: all characters are same. Then the code returns n.

Another case: k is larger than the number of opposite characters. For example, answerKey is "TFFFT", k=2. For 'T' case, the maximum is 4 (flip two F's). The code would compute that.

Yes.

solution: 
```cpp
class Solution {
public:
    int maxConsecutiveAnswers(string answerKey, int k) {
        int maxT = 0, countF = 0, left = 0;
        for (int right = 0; right < answerKey.size(); ++right) {
            if (answerKey[right] == 'F') countF++;
            while (countF > k) {
                if (answerKey[left] == 'F') countF--;
                left++;
            }
            maxT = max(maxT, right - left + 1);
        }
        int maxF = 0, countT = 0, left2 = 0;
        for (int right = 0; right < answerKey.size(); ++right) {
            if (answerKey[right] == 'T') countT++;
            while (countT > k) {
                if (answerKey[left2] == 'T') countT--;
                left2++;
            }
            maxF = max(maxF, right - left2 + 1);
        }
        return max(maxT, maxF);
    }
};
```
efficiency: HIGH
time complexity: O(n)
space complexity: O(1)
energy implications: LOW
explanation: Uses sliding window technique twice (once for maximizing 'T's and once for 'F's). For each window, it tracks the count of the opposite character and adjusts the window size to stay within k allowed changes. Efficiently computes maximum in linear time with constant space.
